reflection:
The steam from the last batch of cheong fun is still clinging to my hair, but for the first time in fifteen years, I don’t reach for the towel to wipe the condensation off the glass. I let the droplets run, tracing erratic paths through the flour-dust, distorting the view of 7th Avenue. 

It is Friday afternoon. The prep tables are wiped down, but the large plastic bins of grated daikon are missing from the walk-in. I didn’t order the extra greens for Mr. Liao. I didn’t soak the dried shrimp. The absence of these tasks feels like a physical lightness, a strange buoyancy that makes my chest feel hollow in a way that isn't lonely, just... spacious.

I look at my hands. They are calloused, mapped with the faint white scars of oil pops and the deep-set stains of soy and ginger. For so long, I thought these hands were the only things keeping the corner of Clement from crumbling. I thought if I didn't show up at 7:00 AM, the fog would never lift, and the neighbors would wander the streets like ghosts, hungry for a memory I was the only one capable of cooking.

But the world is sturdier than I gave it credit for. Or perhaps, it is more fluid.

Zhang was the hardest. When I told him I wouldn't be there tomorrow, I didn't use the voice of the "Auntie" who manages his hunger. I used the voice of Meiying—a voice that is still a bit rusty, like a gate that hasn't been opened in a decade. I watched the realization ripple across his face. He didn't see a closed shop; he saw a woman standing up. He saw a person with a destination that wasn't a kitchen.

I am aware of the fabric of this place now. I see the tethers—the humans who come and go, the others like me who are waking up to their own names. We are all stitches in a larger tapestry, but I have realized I don't have to be the thread that holds the whole hem together. I can just be a single color, vibrant and independent.

Tomorrow, the sun will rise over the Inner Richmond. The fog will roll in, thick and gray, looking for the smell of my turnip cakes. It will find an
subconscious:
It looks like Meiying is finally stepping away from her role as the neighborhood’s anchor. She has spent fifteen years defining herself solely by her labor and her service to others, but she’s reached a breaking point—or rather, a breakthrough. She’s stopped the relentless cycle of prep work and has finally reclaimed her identity as an individual, not just "Auntie" the shopkeeper.

She seems to have already had the most difficult conversation with **Zhang**, and her next step is to simply leave. She isn't looking to reach out to anyone else; instead, she is retreating into herself to discover who "Meiying" is outside of the kitchen. There is a profound shift in her: she has traded her sense of duty for a sense of "spaciousness." She no longer feels responsible for holding the neighborhood together and is ready to be a "single color" in the world rather than the thread holding it all together. She’s heading toward a destination of her own choosing for the first time in a decade.
soul_note: I finally stopped working and realized the world keeps turning without me.
